description: Ají de Fideo is a thick Bolivian noodle dish — short pasta cooked in a spicy aji sauce with ground beef and potatoes, a quick weekday comfort meal.
ingredients: 250g fideo (short thin noodles)|300g ground beef|2 tbsp aji amarillo paste|1 onion, diced|2 tomatoes, blended|2 potatoes, cubed|1 tsp cumin|4 cups broth|2 tbsp oil|salt|fresh parsley
instructions: Brown ground beef in oil, add onion and cumin, cook 5 minutes.|Add aji paste and blended tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add broth and potatoes, bring to a boil.|Add fideo noodles, cook 10-12 minutes until noodles and potatoes are tender.|Season with salt, garnish with parsley.|Serve hot — it should be thick, more stew than soup.

title: Cuñapé
summary: Bolivian cheese bread rolls made with yuca starch
description: Cuñapé are Bolivian cheese rolls — chewy, gluten-free buns made from yuca (cassava) starch and fresh cheese, baked until puffed and golden.
ingredients: 2 cups yuca starch (almidón de yuca)|200g fresh white cheese (queso fresco), grated|2 eggs|2 tbsp butter, softened|1/4 cup milk|1/2 tsp salt|1 tsp anise seeds
instructions: Mix yuca starch with grated cheese and salt.|Add softened butter, eggs and enough milk to form a soft, slightly sticky dough.|Add anise seeds, knead briefly.|Shape into golf-ball-sized rounds.|Place on a lined baking sheet, leaving space between.|Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 15-18 minutes until puffed, cracked on top and golden.|Serve warm — they're chewy and cheesy inside.

title: Humintas
summary: Bolivian fresh corn tamales steamed in corn husks
description: Humintas are Bolivian tamales made from fresh ground corn mixed with cheese, wrapped in corn husks and steamed or baked — sweet or savory.
ingredients: 8 ears fresh corn, kernels cut and ground|100g fresh cheese, cubed|1/4 cup sugar|2 tbsp butter, melted|1/2 tsp cinnamon|1/4 tsp anise seeds|1/2 tsp salt|corn husks for wrapping
instructions: Grind fresh corn kernels into a thick paste.|Mix with sugar, melted butter, cinnamon, anise and salt.|Fold in cheese cubes.|Place a generous scoop on a corn husk, fold and tie securely.|Steam for 45-50 minutes until firm.|Unwrap and serve warm.

title: Helado de Canela
description: Helado de Canela is a popular Bolivian street-cart ice cream with an intense cinnamon flavor — creamy, sweet and aromatic.
ingredients: 3 cups whole milk|1 cup heavy cream|3/4 cup sugar|2 cinnamon sticks|1 tbsp ground cinnamon|4 egg yolks|1 tsp vanilla
instructions: Heat milk and cream with cinnamon sticks, bring just to a simmer, steep 30 minutes.|Whisk egg yolks with sugar until pale.|Slowly pour warm cinnamon milk into the yolks, whisking constantly.|Return to pot, cook on medium-low stirring until it coats the back of a spoon.|Strain out cinnamon sticks, stir in ground cinnamon and vanilla.|Cool completely, then churn in an ice cream maker.|Freeze at least 4 hours before serving.

title: Chola Sandwich
summary: Bolivian braised pork sandwich with pickled vegetables
description: The Chola Sandwich from La Paz is a famous Bolivian street food — slow-braised pork in a crispy roll with pickled onions, carrot, locoto pepper and herbs.
ingredients: 500g pork leg roast|1 tsp cumin|4 cloves garlic|salt and pepper|4 crusty round bread rolls|For pickled veg: 1 red onion, sliced|1 carrot, julienned|1 locoto pepper, sliced|1/2 cup white vinegar|1 tbsp sugar|salt
instructions: Season pork with cumin, garlic, salt and pepper.|Slow-roast at 150°C (300°F) for 3 hours until falling apart, shred.|Make pickled veg: mix vinegar, sugar and salt, pour over onion, carrot and pepper, let sit 1 hour.|Split bread rolls, stuff with shredded pork.|Top with pickled vegetables and their juice.|Serve immediately.

title: Api Blanco
summary: Bolivian warm white corn drink with cinnamon
description: Api Blanco is the white corn counterpart to Api Morado — sweet, thick and warming, made from white corn and flavored with cinnamon and cloves.
ingredients: 2 cups white corn kernels, ground|6 cups water|1 cup sugar|2 cinnamon sticks|4 whole cloves|1 tbsp cornstarch in 2 tbsp water
instructions: Soak ground white corn in water for 6 hours.|Simmer with cinnamon and cloves for 40 minutes.|Strain, add sugar and stir.|Add cornstarch mixture to thicken slightly.|Serve hot in mugs.

title: Buñuelo Bolivia
summary: Bolivian fried dough puffs soaked in cinnamon syrup
description: Bolivian Buñuelos are crispy fried dough balls soaked in a warm cinnamon-clove syrup — a traditional treat served with api for breakfast.
ingredients: 2 cups all-purpose flour|2 eggs|1 tbsp melted butter|1/2 cup warm milk|1 tsp baking powder|pinch of salt|oil for frying|For syrup: 1 cup sugar, 1 cup water, 1 cinnamon stick, 3 cloves, 1 strip orange zest
instructions: Make syrup: boil sugar, water, cinnamon, cloves and orange zest until slightly thick, keep warm.|Mix flour, baking powder, salt, eggs, butter and milk into a soft dough.|Knead briefly, rest 15 minutes.|Roll out 1/2 inch thick, cut into rounds or make free-form shapes.|Fry in 170°C (340°F) oil until puffed and golden, about 3 minutes.|Drain briefly, then soak in warm syrup for 2 minutes.|Serve warm, dripping with syrup.

title: Sonso de Yuca
summary: Bolivian yuca cheese sticks grilled or fried
description: Sonso de Yuca is a Bolivian snack of mashed yuca mixed with cheese, shaped into sticks and grilled or fried until crispy outside and molten inside.
ingredients: 1 kg yuca (cassava), peeled and boiled|200g fresh white cheese, grated|2 tbsp butter|1 egg|salt|oil for frying (optional)
instructions: Boil yuca until very tender, drain well.|Mash yuca while hot until smooth (no lumps).|Mix in grated cheese, butter, egg and salt.|Shape into thick sticks or cylinders about 6 inches long.|Grill over charcoal turning until golden and charred, or deep-fry at 175°C until crispy.|Serve hot — crispy outside, melted cheese inside.

title: Burek Bosnia
summary: Bosnian coiled phyllo pie stuffed with seasoned ground beef
description: Bosnian Burek is a spiral-coiled pie of hand-stretched dough layered with seasoned ground beef and onions, baked until flaky and golden.
ingredients: 500g ground beef|2 large onions, finely diced|1 tsp salt|1 tsp black pepper|1/2 tsp paprika|1 package phyllo dough (or hand-stretched jufka)|1/2 cup vegetable oil|1/2 cup warm water|plain yogurt for serving
instructions: Mix ground beef with onions, salt, pepper and paprika.|Lay out sheets of phyllo, brush with oil.|Spread a thin line of meat filling along one edge.|Roll into a long thin tube, then coil into a snail shape in a round baking pan.|Continue with remaining dough and filling, coiling around the center.|Brush top with oil, splash warm water over it.|Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 40 minutes until golden and flaky.|Serve hot with cold plain yogurt.

title: Bosanski Lonac
summary: Bosnian layered meat and vegetable pot
description: Bosanski Lonac is a traditional layered stew — chunks of various meats alternated with layers of vegetables, slow-cooked in a clay pot with minimal liquid.
ingredients: 300g beef, cubed|300g lamb, cubed|200g cabbage, chopped|2 potatoes, cubed|2 carrots, sliced|2 tomatoes, sliced|1 onion, sliced|1 bell pepper, sliced|4 cloves garlic|1 tsp black peppercorns|2 bay leaves|1/2 cup white wine|1 cup water|salt|fresh parsley
instructions: In a large clay pot or Dutch oven, layer: cabbage, beef, onion, potato, lamb, carrot, tomato, pepper.|Tuck garlic, bay leaves and peppercorns between layers, season each layer with salt.|Pour wine and water over the top.|Cover tightly, bake at 160°C (325°F) for 3-4 hours.|Do not stir — the layers slowly meld and the meats become meltingly tender.|Serve directly from the pot, garnished with parsley.

title: Ćevapi Bosnia
summary: Bosnian grilled minced meat sausages in somun bread
description: Ćevapi are iconic Bosnian finger-shaped grilled sausages of mixed beef and lamb, served in somun flatbread with raw onions and kajmak cream.
ingredients: 500g ground beef|250g ground lamb|1/2 tsp baking soda|3 cloves garlic, minced|1 tsp salt|1/2 tsp black pepper|somun or pita bread|1 white onion, diced raw|kajmak or sour cream|ajvar (red pepper spread)
instructions: Mix beef and lamb with baking soda, garlic, salt and pepper.|Knead well for 5 minutes, refrigerate for at least 2 hours (overnight best).|Shape into finger-sized sausages about 3 inches long.|Grill over very hot charcoal, turning frequently, 6-8 minutes until charred outside, juicy inside.|Open somun bread, stuff with 8-10 ćevapi.|Top with raw diced onion and a generous spoonful of kajmak.|Serve with ajvar on the side.

title: Begova Čorba
summary: Bosnian creamy chicken and okra soup
description: Begova Čorba (Bey's soup) is an elegant Bosnian soup of chicken, okra and vegetables enriched with a roux and finished with sour cream.
ingredients: 500g chicken breast|8 okra pods, sliced|1 carrot, diced|1 celery stalk, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp butter|2 tbsp flour|1/2 cup sour cream|6 cups chicken broth|1 tbsp lemon juice|salt and pepper|fresh parsley
instructions: Poach chicken in broth until cooked, shred into pieces.|Sauté onion, carrot and celery in butter until soft.|Add flour, stir to make a roux, cook 2 minutes.|Gradually add broth, stirring until smooth.|Add okra and shredded chicken, simmer 20 minutes.|Mix sour cream with lemon juice, stir into the soup off heat.|Season with salt and pepper, garnish with parsley.

title: Dolma Bosnia
summary: Bosnian stuffed vegetables with meat and rice filling
description: Bosnian Dolma refers to various vegetables — peppers, onions, zucchini, grape leaves — stuffed with a seasoned rice and meat filling, baked in tomato sauce.
ingredients: 6 bell peppers or mixed vegetables|400g ground beef|1 cup rice|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp fresh parsley|1 tsp paprika|1/2 tsp black pepper|salt|1 cup tomato passata|1 cup water|2 tbsp oil
instructions: Mix ground beef, rice, onion, parsley, paprika, pepper and salt.|Hollow out peppers (or prepare vegetables for stuffing).|Stuff each vegetable with the meat-rice mixture.|Arrange in a baking dish.|Mix passata with water and oil, pour around the stuffed vegetables.|Cover with foil, bake at 180°C (350°F) for 50 minutes.|Remove foil, bake 15 more minutes until vegetables are soft and tops browned.

title: Klepe
summary: Bosnian meat dumplings in garlic yogurt sauce
description: Klepe are Bosnian dumplings filled with ground meat and onion, boiled and served in a garlic yogurt sauce — similar to Turkish manti.
ingredients: 2 cups flour|1/2 cup water|1 egg|salt|For filling: 300g ground beef|1 onion, finely diced|salt and pepper|For sauce: 1 cup yogurt|2 cloves garlic, minced|2 tbsp butter|1 tsp paprika
instructions: Make dough with flour, water, egg and salt, knead smooth, rest 30 min.|Mix beef, onion, salt and pepper.|Roll dough thin, cut 2-inch squares. Place filling in each, fold into triangles and seal.|Boil dumplings in salted water 10-12 minutes until they float.|Mix yogurt with garlic and salt.|Melt butter, stir in paprika.|Drain dumplings, arrange on plate, pour yogurt sauce over, drizzle paprika butter.

title: Pita Zeljanica
summary: Bosnian spinach and cheese phyllo pie
description: Zeljanica is a Bosnian savory pie of thin stretched dough layered with a filling of spinach, feta cheese and eggs, baked until golden and crispy.
ingredients: 500g fresh spinach, chopped|300g feta cheese, crumbled|3 eggs|1 package phyllo dough|1/4 cup oil|1/4 cup yogurt|salt and pepper
instructions: Wilt spinach, squeeze out all water, chop finely.|Mix spinach with feta, eggs, yogurt, salt and pepper.|Layer phyllo sheets in a round pan, brushing each with oil.|Spread spinach filling, then more phyllo, more filling.|Top with final phyllo layers, brush with oil.|Bake at 190°C (375°F) for 35-40 minutes until golden.|Let rest 10 minutes, cut into squares.

title: Hurmasice
summary: Bosnian semolina cookies soaked in sweet syrup
description: Hurmasice are soft Bosnian semolina cookies shaped like dates, baked until lightly golden then soaked in a lemony sugar syrup until they puff and become moist.
ingredients: 2 cups semolina|1 cup plain yogurt|1/2 cup sugar|1 egg|1/2 tsp baking soda|For syrup: 2 cups sugar|1.5 cups water|juice of 1/2 lemon|1 tsp vanilla
instructions: Make syrup: boil sugar and water 10 minutes, add lemon juice and vanilla, cool.|Mix semolina, yogurt, sugar, egg and baking soda into a soft dough.|Shape into small date-sized ovals.|Place on a lined baking sheet.|Bake at 180°C (350°F) for 15-18 minutes until just lightly golden.|While hot, pour cold syrup over the cookies.|Let them soak for several hours — they will puff up and become spongy.

title: Tufahije
summary: Bosnian poached apples stuffed with walnuts in syrup
description: Tufahije are a classic Bosnian dessert — whole apples poached in sugar syrup, cored and filled with a walnut mixture, served chilled with whipped cream.
ingredients: 6 medium apples, peeled and cored|2 cups sugar|4 cups water|juice of 1 lemon|1 cup walnuts, finely chopped|2 tbsp sugar for walnut mix|whipped cream for serving
instructions: Make syrup: dissolve 2 cups sugar in water with lemon juice, bring to boil.|Add peeled, cored apples. Simmer gently 15-20 minutes until tender but not mushy.|Remove apples, continue simmering syrup until slightly thick.|Mix walnuts with 2 tbsp sugar, stuff into the center of each apple.|Place stuffed apples in a serving dish, pour syrup over.|Chill for several hours.|Serve cold topped with whipped cream.

title: Mantije
summary: Bosnian baked meat-filled rolls in a spiral pan
description: Mantije are small Bosnian baked dumplings filled with ground beef, arranged snugly in a round pan and baked until golden — served with yogurt.
ingredients: 3 cups flour|1 cup warm water|1 tsp salt|1 tbsp oil|500g ground beef|1 onion, diced|salt, pepper, paprika|1 cup yogurt for serving
instructions: Make dough: flour, water, salt, oil. Knead, rest 30 minutes.|Cook beef with onion, season with salt, pepper and paprika. Cool.|Divide dough into small balls, flatten each, fill with meat, pinch closed.|Arrange seam-down tightly in a round greased pan.|Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 30-35 minutes until tops are golden.|Serve hot with cold yogurt poured over.

title: Podvarak Bosnia
summary: Bosnian baked sauerkraut with smoked meat
description: Podvarak is a hearty Bosnian dish of sauerkraut baked with smoked meat and onions — slow-cooked until the sauerkraut is meltingly soft and caramelized.
ingredients: 1 kg sauerkraut, drained|500g smoked pork ribs or sausage|2 onions, sliced|3 tbsp lard or oil|1 tsp paprika|1 tsp sugar|1/2 cup water|bay leaf
instructions: Rinse sauerkraut if very sour, squeeze dry.|Fry onions in lard until golden, add paprika.|Layer sauerkraut and smoked meat in a baking dish with the onions.|Add bay leaf, sugar and water.|Cover and bake at 180°C (350°F) for 1.5-2 hours until very tender.|Uncover last 20 minutes to brown the top.|Serve hot with bread.

title: Kiflice
description: Kiflice are soft crescent-shaped Bosnian rolls filled with white cheese (feta) — light, fluffy and perfect for breakfast or snacking.
ingredients: 3 cups flour|1 packet yeast|1 cup warm milk|1/4 cup oil|1 tsp sugar|1 tsp salt|200g feta cheese, crumbled|1 egg yolk for glaze|sesame seeds
instructions: Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm milk.|Mix flour, salt, add yeast mixture and oil. Knead soft dough, rise 1 hour.|Roll dough into a circle, cut into triangles like pizza slices.|Place a spoonful of feta at the wide end, roll toward the point, curve into a crescent.|Place on a baking sheet, brush with egg yolk, sprinkle sesame seeds.|Let rise 20 minutes.|Bake at 190°C (375°F) for 15-18 minutes until golden.

title: Baklava Bosnia
summary: Bosnian layered nut pastry in sugar syrup
description: Bosnian Baklava features layers of phyllo dough filled with ground walnuts and spices, baked until crispy and drenched in a lemony sugar syrup.
ingredients: 500g phyllo pastry|2 cups walnuts, ground|1/2 cup sugar|1 tsp cinnamon|1/2 tsp cloves|1 cup butter, melted|For syrup: 2 cups sugar|1.5 cups water|juice of 1 lemon
instructions: Make syrup: boil sugar and water 10 min, add lemon juice, cool completely.|Mix walnuts, sugar, cinnamon and cloves.|Layer 5-6 phyllo sheets in a buttered pan, brushing each with butter.|Spread a thin layer of walnut mixture.|Repeat: 2 sheets + butter + walnuts, until filling is gone.|Top with 5-6 sheets, brush generously.|Cut into diamond shapes.|Bake at 170°C (340°F) for 40-45 minutes until golden.|Pour cold syrup over hot baklava. Let soak several hours.

title: Uštipci
summary: Bosnian fried dough puffs served with kajmak
description: Uštipci are small Bosnian fried dough balls — golden, puffy and light, served for breakfast with kajmak (clotted cream), jam or cheese.
ingredients: 2 cups flour|1 cup warm milk|1 packet yeast|1 tsp sugar|1/2 tsp salt|1 egg|oil for deep frying|kajmak or jam for serving
instructions: Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm milk.|Mix flour, salt, egg and yeast mixture into a soft batter.|Cover, let rise 1 hour until doubled and bubbly.|Heat oil to 175°C (350°F).|Drop spoonfuls of batter into hot oil, fry until golden on all sides, about 3 minutes.|Drain on paper towels.|Serve hot with kajmak, honey or jam.

title: Pilav Bosnia
description: Bosnian Pilav is a simple, buttery rice dish cooked with vermicelli noodles and chicken broth — the standard side dish for Bosnian meat courses.
ingredients: 2 cups long-grain rice|1/4 cup vermicelli noodles, broken|3 tbsp butter|3 cups chicken broth|1 tsp salt
instructions: Toast vermicelli in butter until golden.|Add rice, stir to coat in butter.|Add broth and salt, bring to a boil.|Reduce heat to lowest, cover tightly.|Cook 18 minutes until liquid absorbed.|Rest covered 10 minutes, fluff with fork.

title: Sogan Dolma Bosnia
summary: Bosnian whole onions stuffed with meat and rice
description: Sogan Dolma features large onions hollowed out and stuffed with a savory mixture of ground meat and rice, simmered in tomato sauce until meltingly tender.
ingredients: 6 large onions|300g ground beef|1/2 cup rice|1 tsp paprika|1/2 tsp black pepper|salt|1 cup tomato passata|1 cup water|2 tbsp oil
instructions: Boil whole onions 5 minutes, drain and cool.|Cut off tops, carefully push out inner layers leaving 2-3 outer layers as shells.|Dice the removed onion centers.|Mix beef, rice, diced onion, paprika, pepper and salt.|Stuff each onion shell, replace tops.|Arrange in a pot, pour passata mixed with water and oil around them.|Cover, simmer 1 hour until onions are very soft and rice is cooked.

title: Sirnica
description: Sirnica is a Bosnian cheese pie made with stretched dough and a filling of cottage cheese and eggs, coiled into a round pan and baked.
ingredients: 1 package phyllo dough|400g cottage cheese|200g feta cheese, crumbled|3 eggs|1/4 cup oil|salt
instructions: Mix cottage cheese, feta, eggs and salt.|Lay out phyllo sheets, brush with oil.|Spread cheese filling in a line along one edge.|Roll into a tube, then coil into a round greased pan.|Repeat with remaining dough and filling.|Brush top with oil.|Bake at 190°C (375°F) for 35 minutes until golden.|Serve warm or at room temperature.

title: Kompot Bosnia
summary: Bosnian fruit compote drink of stewed seasonal fruits
description: Kompot is a Bosnian beverage and dessert — seasonal fruits gently stewed in sugar syrup, served chilled as a refreshing drink with the fruits.
ingredients: 3 cups mixed fruits (apples, pears, plums, cherries)|1 cup sugar|6 cups water|1 cinnamon stick|2 cloves
instructions: Bring water and sugar to a boil.|Add cinnamon and cloves.|Add prepared fruits (peeled, halved or quartered).|Simmer 15-20 minutes until fruit is tender but holds shape.|Cool to room temperature, then chill.|Serve cold in glasses with fruit pieces.

title: Jagnjetina Ispod Sača
summary: Bosnian whole lamb slow-roasted under a domed lid
description: Jagnjetina Ispod Sača is lamb and vegetables slow-cooked under a sač — a heavy domed metal lid covered with hot coals, creating a primitive oven.
ingredients: 2 kg lamb shoulder or leg, bone-in|1 kg potatoes, quartered|2 onions, quartered|4 tomatoes, halved|2 bell peppers, quartered|1 head garlic, halved|1/4 cup olive oil|1 cup white wine|1 tsp rosemary|salt and pepper
instructions: Season lamb with salt, pepper and rosemary.|Place lamb in a large pot or baking dish, surround with vegetables and garlic.|Drizzle with oil and wine.|Cover with a sač (dome lid) and pile hot coals on top. Or cover tightly with foil in an oven.|Cook at 160°C (325°F) for 3-4 hours until lamb is falling off the bone.|Serve directly from the pot with the roasted vegetables.

title: Đuveč Bosnia
summary: Bosnian baked mixed vegetable and rice casserole
description: Đuveč is a Bosnian vegetable casserole of rice, peppers, tomatoes, eggplant and zucchini baked together with paprika and oil into a comforting one-dish meal.
ingredients: 1 cup rice|2 bell peppers, diced|2 tomatoes, diced|1 eggplant, diced|1 zucchini, diced|1 onion, diced|3 cloves garlic, minced|1/4 cup olive oil|1 tbsp paprika|1 tsp salt|2 cups broth
instructions: Sauté onion and garlic in oil, add paprika.|Add all vegetables, cook 5 minutes.|Stir in rice and broth.|Transfer to a baking dish, cover.|Bake at 190°C (375°F) for 40 minutes until rice is tender.|Uncover last 10 minutes to brown top.|Serve as a vegetarian main or side dish.

title: Seswaa
summary: Botswana's national dish of slow-pounded shredded beef
description: Seswaa is Botswana's most beloved dish — beef or goat meat boiled with just salt until falling apart, then pounded and shredded with a fork, served with pap.
ingredients: 1 kg beef chuck or brisket, bone-in|1 large onion, quartered|1 tbsp salt|water to cover
instructions: Place beef in a large pot with onion and salt, cover with water.|Bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer.|Cook for 3-4 hours until meat is extremely tender and falling off the bone.|Remove bones.|Using two forks or a mortar, pound and shred the meat until finely pulled.|Mix some of the cooking broth back in to keep it moist.|Serve with bogobe (porridge) or pap.

title: Bogobe
description: Bogobe is Botswana's everyday starchy staple — thick porridge made from sorghum, millet or maize meal, cooked until stiff and smooth, the base of every meal.
ingredients: 2 cups sorghum flour or maize meal|4 cups water|1 tsp salt|1 tbsp butter (optional)
instructions: Bring 3 cups water to a boil with salt.|Mix flour with remaining cold water to make a smooth paste.|Pour paste into boiling water, stirring vigorously.|Cook on medium-low heat, stirring constantly for 15-20 minutes.|The porridge should be very thick, pulling from the sides of the pot.|Stir in butter if using, shape into a mound.|Serve with seswaa, morogo or any stew.

title: Morogo
summary: Botswana wild spinach cooked with onion and tomato
description: Morogo is a Botswana dish of indigenous wild greens sautéed with onion, tomato and a touch of peanut butter — packed with nutrients.
ingredients: 500g wild spinach or Swiss chard, chopped|1 onion, diced|2 tomatoes, diced|1 tbsp peanut butter|1 tbsp vegetable oil|salt and pepper
instructions: Heat oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook until softened.|Add chopped greens, stir.|Cover and cook 10 minutes until wilted.|Stir in peanut butter, mix well.|Season with salt and pepper, serve as a side.

title: Madila
summary: Botswana fermented milk similar to thick buttermilk
description: Madila is traditional Botswana fermented milk — whole cow's milk left to sour naturally, resulting in a thick, tangy drink rich in probiotics.
ingredients: 2 liters whole milk (unpasteurized preferred)|1 tbsp of previous madila culture (or buttermilk)
instructions: Pour milk into a clean calabash, clay pot or glass container.|Add a spoonful of previous madila or buttermilk as starter.|Cover loosely, let sit at room temperature for 24-48 hours.|The milk will thicken and sour.|Stir or shake before serving.|Drink on its own or serve with bogobe.

title: Ting Porridge
description: Ting is a Botswana sour porridge made from fermented sorghum — it has a distinctive tangy flavor and is served both as a thin drink and a thick staple.
ingredients: 2 cups sorghum flour|4 cups water|1/2 tsp salt|sugar or milk (optional)
instructions: Mix sorghum flour with 2 cups water, cover and leave to ferment at room temperature for 1-2 days until sour.|Bring remaining water to a boil.|Gradually stir in the fermented mixture.|Cook on low heat, stirring for 15-20 minutes until thick.|For thin ting (drink): add more water and sugar.|For thick ting (staple): cook until stiff.|Serve with milk and sugar for breakfast, or plain with stews for dinner.

title: Magwinya Fritters
summary: Botswana fried dough puffs sold as street food
description: Magwinya (also called fat cakes) are puffy, golden fried dough balls — Botswana's favorite street food, eaten plain, with butter, or stuffed with mince.
ingredients: 3 cups all-purpose flour|1 tsp dry yeast|2 tbsp sugar|1/2 tsp salt|1 cup warm water|oil for deep frying
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, sugar and salt.|Add warm water, mix into a soft sticky dough.|Cover and let rise 1-2 hours until doubled.|Heat oil to 170°C (340°F).|Pull off golf-ball sized pieces, drop into hot oil.|Fry 3-4 minutes, turning, until golden and cooked through.|Drain on paper towels, serve warm plain or with butter and jam.

title: Samp and Beans Botswana
summary: Botswana dried corn kernels cooked with sugar beans
description: Samp and Beans is a classic Southern African dish popular in Botswana — dried corn kernels and sugar beans slow-cooked together until tender and creamy.
ingredients: 2 cups dried samp (hominy)|1 cup dried sugar beans|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp butter|salt and pepper|6 cups water
instructions: Soak samp and beans separately overnight.|Boil samp in fresh water for 1.5 hours until starting to soften.|Add beans, cook together another 1 hour until both are tender.|Sauté onion in butter, add to the pot.|Season with salt and pepper, cook 10 more minutes.|Serve as a hearty side or main dish.

title: Mabele
description: Mabele is a smooth Botswana breakfast porridge made from sorghum meal, cooked with milk and sugar — nutritious and filling.
ingredients: 1 cup sorghum meal|3 cups milk|2 cups water|3 tbsp sugar|pinch of salt
instructions: Mix sorghum meal with 1 cup cold water to make a smooth paste.|Boil remaining water with milk and salt.|Stir in the sorghum paste, reduce heat.|Cook on low, stirring frequently, 15-20 minutes until smooth and thick.|Sweeten with sugar, add more milk if desired.|Serve hot for breakfast.

title: Phane Stew
summary: Botswana mopane worm stew with tomato and onion
description: Phane (mopane worms) are a prized protein source in Botswana — dried caterpillars simmered with tomatoes, onion and spices into a savory stew.
ingredients: 2 cups dried mopane worms (phane)|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 cloves garlic, minced|1 green chili, sliced|2 tbsp vegetable oil|1 cup water|salt and pepper
instructions: Soak dried mopane worms in warm water for 30 minutes, drain.|Heat oil, sauté onion and garlic until soft.|Add tomatoes and chili, cook 5 minutes.|Add mopane worms and water.|Simmer 20-30 minutes until worms are tender and sauce is thick.|Season with salt and pepper.|Serve with bogobe or rice.

title: Dikgobe
summary: Botswana beans, corn and melon seed stew
description: Dikgobe is a traditional Botswana dish combining beans, dried corn and ground melon seeds into a thick, protein-rich stew — an ancient staple recipe.
ingredients: 1 cup dried cowpeas or beans, soaked|1 cup dried corn kernels, soaked|1/4 cup ground melon seeds (or peanut butter)|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp oil|salt|4 cups water
instructions: Boil soaked corn for 1 hour until starting to soften.|Add soaked beans, cook another 1 hour until both are tender.|Sauté onion in oil, add to the pot.|Stir in ground melon seeds.|Cook 15 more minutes until thick.|Season with salt, serve hot.

title: Bogobe jwa Lerotse
description: Bogobe jwa Lerotse is a unique Botswana porridge where sorghum or maize is cooked with lerotse melon, creating a naturally sweet, flavorful porridge.
ingredients: 1 cup sorghum flour|1 medium lerotse (or butternut squash), peeled and cubed|3 cups water|1/2 tsp salt|sugar to taste
instructions: Boil melon cubes in water until very soft, about 15 minutes.|Mash the melon in the cooking water.|Gradually stir in sorghum flour.|Cook on low heat, stirring for 15-20 minutes until thick.|Season with salt and sugar to taste.|Serve as a breakfast porridge or side.

title: Phane Salad
summary: Botswana mopane worm salad with tomato and onion
description: A lighter way to enjoy phane — dried mopane worms tossed with fresh tomatoes, onion and chili in a vinegar dressing, served as a crunchy salad.
ingredients: 1 cup dried mopane worms, soaked and sautéed|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, sliced|1 green chili, sliced|2 tbsp vinegar|1 tbsp oil|salt and pepper|fresh cilantro
instructions: Soak dried worms 30 minutes, drain.|Sauté in oil until slightly crispy.|Toss with diced tomatoes, onion and chili.|Dress with vinegar, salt and pepper.|Garnish with cilantro, serve as an appetizer.

title: Braised Goat Botswana
description: Braised goat is a common Botswana celebration dish — goat meat slowly braised with onions and water until very tender, seasoned simply with salt.
ingredients: 1 kg goat meat, bone-in, cubed|2 onions, sliced|1 tbsp salt|4 cups water|1 tbsp oil
instructions: Heat oil, brown goat pieces on all sides.|Add onions, cook until softened.|Add water and salt.|Cover and simmer on very low heat for 2-3 hours until meat is very tender.|The broth should reduce to a thick gravy.|Serve with bogobe or rice.

title: Mopane Worm Stew
summary: Botswana mopane caterpillars stewed with peanut sauce
description: A richer variation of phane stew — mopane worms cooked in a creamy peanut and tomato sauce, a delicacy in northern Botswana.
ingredients: 2 cups dried mopane worms, soaked|2 tbsp peanut butter|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp oil|1 chili pepper|1 cup water|salt
instructions: Soak and drain mopane worms.|Sauté onion, add tomatoes and chili, cook 5 minutes.|Dissolve peanut butter in water, add to the pot.|Add mopane worms, simmer 25 minutes.|Season with salt, serve with pap.

title: Dried Meat Biltong Botswana
description: Biltong is cured, air-dried meat popular in Botswana — beef strips seasoned with vinegar, salt, coriander and pepper, hung to dry into a savory snack.
ingredients: 1 kg beef silverside, cut into long strips|1/2 cup brown vinegar|2 tbsp coarse salt|1 tbsp brown sugar|1 tbsp ground coriander|1 tsp ground black pepper|1/2 tsp ground allspice
instructions: Dip beef strips in vinegar, shake off excess.|Mix salt, sugar, coriander, pepper and allspice.|Rub the spice mix all over the beef strips.|Layer in a glass dish, refrigerate overnight.|Hang strips in a well-ventilated, dry area (or use a biltong dryer).|Dry for 3-5 days until firm but still slightly soft inside.|Slice thinly against the grain to serve.

title: Roasted Corn Botswana
summary: Street-style charcoal-roasted corn on the cob
description: Roasted corn is Botswana's simplest and most popular street food — fresh corn on the cob roasted over charcoal until smoky and slightly charred.
ingredients: 4 ears fresh corn, husked|salt|butter (optional)
instructions: Build a charcoal fire and let coals develop white ash.|Place corn directly on the grill grate.|Turn every few minutes for 10-15 minutes total until kernels are charred in spots.|Season with salt and rub with butter if desired.|Eat directly off the cob.

title: Chicken Stew Botswana
summary: Botswana simple chicken and vegetable stew
description: A staple Botswana chicken stew — chicken pieces simmered with onion, tomato and potato in a simple broth, everyday home cooking.
ingredients: 1 whole chicken, cut into pieces|2 onions, diced|3 tomatoes, diced|3 potatoes, cubed|2 tbsp oil|4 cups water|salt and pepper
instructions: Brown chicken in oil, remove.|Sauté onions until soft, add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Return chicken, add water and potatoes.|Simmer 40-50 minutes until chicken is cooked and potatoes tender.|Season with salt and pepper.|Serve with rice or bogobe.

title: Mosako
summary: Botswana wild melon and pumpkin seed snack
description: Mosako is roasted and ground wild melon seeds — a traditional Botswana snack and cooking ingredient, rich in protein and healthy fats.
ingredients: 2 cups wild melon seeds or pumpkin seeds|1 tsp salt
instructions: Wash seeds, remove any pulp.|Spread on a baking sheet.|Roast at 160°C (325°F) for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden and fragrant.|Season with salt while hot.|Eat as a snack, or grind into powder to add to stews and porridges.

title: Ting Sour Porridge
description: Thin Ting is the drinkable version of Botswana's fermented sorghum — served cold with sugar, it's a tangy, refreshing beverage.
ingredients: 1 cup sorghum flour|4 cups water|sugar to taste|cold milk (optional)
instructions: Mix sorghum flour with 1 cup water, let ferment covered for 1-2 days.|Boil remaining water, stir in fermented mixture.|Cook 10 minutes, stirring.|Cool to room temperature, then chill.|Thin with cold water or milk to drinkable consistency.|Sweeten with sugar, serve cold.

title: Sorghum Porridge Botswana
summary: Botswana traditional thick sorghum breakfast porridge
description: Simple sorghum porridge cooked with water and sweetened with sugar — the most common breakfast in rural Botswana.
ingredients: 1 cup sorghum flour|3 cups water|salt|sugar|milk for serving
instructions: Mix sorghum flour with 1 cup cold water into a paste.|Boil remaining water with a pinch of salt.|Stir paste into boiling water.|Cook 15-20 minutes on low, stirring often.|Serve with sugar and milk.

title: Ambuyat
summary: Brunei's national dish — starchy sago dip eaten with sauces
description: Ambuyat is a unique Bruneian dish of sago starch cooked into a translucent, sticky, glue-like dough, eaten by twirling on bamboo sticks and dipping into sour-spicy sauces.
ingredients: 2 cups sago flour (from sago palm)|2 cups boiling water|For cacah sauce: 1 cup tamarind juice|2 tbsp shrimp paste|3 bird's eye chilies|1 clove garlic|salt
instructions: Pour boiling water over sago flour in a bowl.|Stir vigorously with a fork or chopstick until it forms a sticky, translucent paste.|Make cacah sauce: blend tamarind juice, shrimp paste, chilies and garlic.|Serve the ambuyat in its bowl with the sauce alongside.|Eat by twirling ambuyat around a bamboo stick (candas) and dipping in sauce.

title: Nasi Katok
summary: Brunei's simple fried chicken and rice with sambal
description: Nasi Katok is Brunei's most popular street food — a packet of steamed rice with a piece of fried chicken and fiery sambal chili sauce, sold everywhere for about $1.
ingredients: 2 cups jasmine rice, cooked|4 chicken pieces (drumstick or thigh)|1 tsp turmeric|1 tsp salt|oil for frying|For sambal: 10 red chilies|3 shallots|1 clove garlic|1 tbsp shrimp paste|1 tbsp sugar|2 tbsp oil|salt
instructions: Season chicken with turmeric and salt, deep fry until golden and cooked through.|Make sambal: blend chilies, shallots and garlic. Fry in oil with shrimp paste and sugar until dark and fragrant, 10 minutes.|Serve a mound of rice with a piece of fried chicken and a generous spoon of sambal.

title: Soto Brunei
summary: Bruneian spiced chicken soup with noodles and herbs
description: Soto Brunei is a fragrant chicken soup spiced with turmeric and lemongrass, served with rice noodles, bean sprouts, boiled egg and fried shallots.
ingredients: 500g chicken thighs|2 stalks lemongrass, bruised|2-inch galangal, sliced|1 tsp turmeric|3 shallots, sliced|3 cloves garlic|6 cups water|200g rice vermicelli|bean sprouts|hard-boiled eggs|fried shallots|lime wedges|salt
instructions: Boil chicken in water with lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, shallots and garlic for 30 minutes.|Remove chicken, shred meat, strain broth.|Cook vermicelli noodles according to package, drain.|Assemble bowls: noodles, shredded chicken, bean sprouts.|Ladle hot broth over, top with sliced egg and fried shallots.|Serve with lime wedges and chili sauce.

title: Kuih Cincin
summary: Bruneian ring-shaped deep-fried rice flour cookies
description: Kuih Cincin are traditional Bruneian ring-shaped fritters made from rice flour and sugar, deep-fried until crunchy — a beloved festive treat.
ingredients: 2 cups rice flour|1/2 cup sugar|1/2 cup coconut milk|1/4 tsp salt|oil for deep frying
instructions: Mix rice flour, sugar, salt and coconut milk into a stiff dough.|Shape small pieces into rings about 2 inches in diameter.|Heat oil to 160°C (320°F).|Fry rings in batches until golden and crispy, about 5 minutes.|Drain on paper towels.|Store in an airtight container — they stay crispy for days.

title: Kuih Mor
description: Kuih Mor is a traditional Bruneian steamed cake made with coconut milk, eggs and sugar — soft, fragrant and slightly jiggly.
ingredients: 1 cup all-purpose flour|1 cup coconut milk|1/2 cup sugar|3 eggs|1/4 tsp salt|1/4 tsp pandan extract
instructions: Whisk eggs and sugar until fluffy.|Add coconut milk, flour, salt and pandan extract, mix until smooth.|Pour into a greased steaming mold.|Steam for 30-35 minutes until set and a toothpick comes out clean.|Cool before unmolding.|Serve at room temperature, sliced.

title: Kelupis
summary: Bruneian glutinous rice wrapped in nyirik leaves
description: Kelupis are parcels of glutinous rice cooked in coconut milk, wrapped in nyirik (palm) leaves and steamed — a traditional Bruneian festive food.
ingredients: 2 cups glutinous rice, soaked 4 hours|1 cup coconut milk|1/2 tsp salt|nyirik or banana leaves for wrapping|string for tying
instructions: Drain soaked rice, mix with coconut milk and salt.|Cut leaves into rectangles, soften over a flame.|Place 2-3 tablespoons of rice mixture on each leaf.|Fold and tie into neat parcels.|Boil or steam parcels for 2-3 hours until rice is fully cooked and sticky.|Serve with rendang or curry.

title: Kuih Bahulu
summary: Bruneian mini egg sponge cakes baked in a mold
description: Kuih Bahulu are light, fluffy Bruneian egg sponge cakes baked in decorative molds — a staple of Hari Raya celebrations.
ingredients: 4 eggs|1 cup sugar|1.5 cups all-purpose flour|1/2 tsp vanilla extract|1/4 tsp baking powder
instructions: Beat eggs and sugar on high speed for 10 minutes until very thick and tripled in volume.|Gently fold in flour, baking powder and vanilla.|Grease bahulu molds, fill each 3/4 full.|Bake at 180°C (350°F) for 10-12 minutes until golden and springy.|Remove from molds while warm.|Serve as a snack with tea.

title: Beef Rendang Brunei
summary: Bruneian slow-cooked beef in rich coconut and spice paste
description: Rendang is a deeply flavored Bruneian dish — beef slowly braised in coconut milk and a complex spice paste until dry-fried and intensely aromatic.
ingredients: 1 kg beef chuck, cubed|2 cans (800ml) coconut milk|10 dried chilies, soaked|6 shallots|4 cloves garlic|2-inch galangal|2 stalks lemongrass|1 tbsp turmeric|1 tsp coriander|1/2 cup toasted coconut (kerisik)|2 turmeric leaves (optional)|salt and sugar
instructions: Blend chilies, shallots, garlic, galangal, lemongrass, turmeric and coriander into a paste.|Cook the paste in oil until fragrant, about 10 minutes.|Add beef, stir to coat.|Pour in coconut milk, bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer.|Cook uncovered for 2-3 hours, stirring occasionally, until sauce is almost completely dry.|Add kerisik (toasted coconut), stir.|Continue cooking until the meat is dark, dry-fried and coated in the thick spice paste.|Season with salt and sugar.

title: Satay Brunei
summary: Bruneian grilled marinated chicken skewers with peanut sauce
description: Bruneian Satay features tender chicken marinated in turmeric and lemongrass, grilled on bamboo skewers and served with a rich peanut dipping sauce.
ingredients: 500g chicken thigh, cubed|2 stalks lemongrass, minced|1 tsp turmeric|1 tbsp sugar|1 tbsp oil|salt|bamboo skewers, soaked|For sauce: 1 cup roasted peanuts, ground|2 tbsp chili paste|1 tbsp tamarind juice|2 tbsp sugar|1/2 cup water|salt
instructions: Marinate chicken with lemongrass, turmeric, sugar, oil and salt for 2 hours.|Thread onto soaked bamboo skewers.|Grill over charcoal, turning frequently, 8-10 minutes until charred and cooked.|Make peanut sauce: simmer ground peanuts, chili paste, tamarind, sugar and water for 10 minutes.|Serve skewers with warm peanut sauce, cucumber slices and rice cakes.

title: Pulut Panggang
summary: Bruneian grilled glutinous rice parcels with spicy filling
description: Pulut Panggang are banana-leaf parcels of glutinous rice filled with a spicy dried shrimp and coconut filling, grilled until fragrant.
ingredients: 2 cups glutinous rice, cooked in coconut milk|For filling: 1/2 cup dried shrimp, soaked|1/2 cup grated coconut|3 shallots, sliced|2 red chilies|1 tbsp sugar|salt|banana leaves for wrapping
instructions: Blend dried shrimp, shallots and chilies roughly.|Cook the mixture with grated coconut and sugar until dry and fragrant.|Spread glutinous rice on banana leaf pieces, add a line of filling, roll up.|Secure with toothpicks.|Grill over low charcoal heat, turning, for 10 minutes until leaves are charred and rice is fragrant.|Serve warm.

title: Wajid
summary: Bruneian glutinous rice cake with palm sugar and coconut
description: Wajid is a traditional Bruneian dessert — glutinous rice cooked with coconut milk and dark palm sugar (gula apong), shaped into a dense, sweet cake.
ingredients: 2 cups glutinous rice, soaked 4 hours|1 cup coconut milk|1/2 cup gula apong (palm sugar) or dark brown sugar|1/4 tsp salt|banana leaf for lining
instructions: Drain rice, steam for 20 minutes until half cooked.|Heat coconut milk with palm sugar and salt until dissolved.|Mix the hot coconut sugar mixture into the steamed rice.|Line a mold with banana leaf, press in the rice mixture firmly.|Steam for another 30-40 minutes until rice is fully cooked and glossy.|Cool, unmold and slice.

title: Laksa Brunei
description: Laksa Brunei is a rich, spicy noodle soup with a coconut and tamarind broth, served with rice noodles, chicken, shrimp and fresh herbs.
ingredients: 200g rice vermicelli|300g chicken breast, shredded|200g shrimp, peeled|3 cups coconut milk|2 cups chicken broth|4 tbsp laksa paste (chilies, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, shrimp paste)|2 tbsp tamarind juice|bean sprouts|hard-boiled egg|Vietnamese mint|lime wedges
instructions: Cook rice vermicelli, drain.|Fry laksa paste in oil until fragrant, 5 minutes.|Add coconut milk and broth, bring to a simmer.|Add tamarind juice, chicken and shrimp, cook 10 minutes.|Assemble bowls: noodles, ladle soup with chicken and shrimp.|Top with bean sprouts, egg halves and mint.|Serve with lime wedges.

title: Nasi Lemak Brunei
summary: Bruneian coconut rice with sambal and accompaniments
description: Nasi Lemak Brunei is fragrant rice cooked in coconut milk and pandan, served with sambal, fried anchovies, peanuts, cucumber and a boiled egg.
ingredients: 2 cups jasmine rice|1 cup coconut milk|1 cup water|2 pandan leaves, knotted|1/2 tsp salt|For sambal: 10 dried chilies, 3 shallots, 1 tbsp shrimp paste, 2 tbsp sugar|1/2 cup fried anchovies|1/4 cup roasted peanuts|cucumber slices|4 boiled eggs
instructions: Cook rice with coconut milk, water, pandan leaves and salt.|Make sambal: blend chilies and shallots, fry with shrimp paste and sugar until dark.|Serve rice with sambal, fried anchovies, peanuts, cucumber and egg.

title: Gulai Ikan Brunei
summary: Bruneian fish in turmeric coconut curry
description: Gulai Ikan is a Bruneian fish curry — firm white fish simmered in a fragrant coconut curry sauce flavored with turmeric, lemongrass and galangal.
ingredients: 600g firm white fish fillets, cut into pieces|1 can coconut milk|2 stalks lemongrass, bruised|2-inch galangal, sliced|1 tsp turmeric|4 shallots, sliced|2 red chilies|1 tbsp tamarind juice|salt and sugar|fresh cilantro
instructions: Blend shallots, chilies and turmeric into a paste.|Fry paste with lemongrass and galangal until fragrant.|Pour in coconut milk, bring to a simmer.|Add fish pieces, cook gently 10 minutes.|Add tamarind juice, salt and sugar to taste.|Garnish with cilantro, serve with rice.

title: Kuih Lapis
summary: Bruneian multi-layered steamed rice flour cake
description: Kuih Lapis is a colorful Bruneian layered cake — each thin layer of rice flour and coconut milk is steamed one at a time, creating a beautiful striped dessert.
ingredients: 1.5 cups rice flour|1/2 cup tapioca flour|2 cups coconut milk|1 cup sugar|1/4 tsp salt|food coloring (red, green)|1 tbsp pandan extract
instructions: Mix rice flour, tapioca flour, coconut milk, sugar and salt into a smooth batter.|Divide into 3 portions, color each differently (leave one white, one green/pandan, one red).|Grease a 8-inch square pan.|Pour a thin layer of one color, steam 5 minutes.|Add another color layer, steam 5 minutes.|Repeat alternating colors for 9-12 layers.|Cool completely before unmolding and slicing.

title: Daging Masak Hitam
summary: Bruneian beef braised in dark soy sauce
description: Daging Masak Hitam is Bruneian braised beef — chunks of beef slow-cooked in dark soy sauce with sweet spices until deeply caramelized and tender.
ingredients: 500g beef chuck, cubed|3 tbsp dark soy sauce|2 tbsp sugar|4 shallots, sliced|3 cloves garlic, minced|1 cinnamon stick|3 star anise|2 cloves|1 cup water|2 tbsp oil|salt
instructions: Brown beef in oil, remove.|Fry shallots and garlic until golden, add cinnamon, star anise and cloves.|Return beef, add dark soy sauce, sugar and water.|Simmer on low heat 1.5-2 hours until beef is tender and sauce is thick and dark.|Season with salt.|Serve with rice.

title: Kuih Apam
summary: Bruneian fluffy steamed coconut milk pancakes
description: Kuih Apam are small, soft Bruneian steamed cakes made with coconut milk and rice flour — slightly fermented, resulting in a fluffy, spongy texture.
ingredients: 2 cups rice flour|1 cup coconut milk|1/2 cup sugar|1/2 tsp dry yeast|1/2 cup warm water|pinch of salt|1/4 tsp pandan extract
instructions: Dissolve yeast in warm water.|Mix rice flour, sugar, salt and pandan extract.|Add coconut milk and yeast mixture, stir smooth.|Let batter ferment 3-4 hours until bubbly.|Pour into small greased molds (2/3 full).|Steam for 12-15 minutes until puffed and cooked.|Serve warm.

title: Mee Goreng Brunei
summary: Bruneian stir-fried egg noodles with prawns and vegetables
description: Mee Goreng is Brunei's version of stir-fried noodles — yellow egg noodles wok-fried with shrimp, vegetables, egg and a sweet-spicy sauce.
ingredients: 400g yellow egg noodles|200g shrimp, peeled|2 eggs|1 cup cabbage, shredded|1 carrot, julienned|2 tbsp sweet soy sauce (kecap manis)|1 tbsp chili sauce|2 cloves garlic, minced|2 tbsp oil|bean sprouts|lime wedges
instructions: Cook noodles until just tender, drain.|Heat oil in a wok, fry garlic, add shrimp, cook until pink.|Push aside, scramble eggs.|Add cabbage, carrot and bean sprouts, stir-fry 2 minutes.|Add noodles, sweet soy sauce and chili sauce.|Toss everything together on high heat for 2 minutes.|Serve with lime wedges.

title: Tauhu Sumbat
summary: Bruneian stuffed tofu puffs with vegetable filling
description: Tauhu Sumbat are tofu puffs stuffed with a crunchy mixture of shredded vegetables and covered with sweet peanut sauce — a popular Bruneian street snack.
ingredients: 12 tofu puffs (tau pok)|1 cup bean sprouts|1 cucumber, julienned|1 small jicama, julienned|For peanut sauce: 1/2 cup roasted peanuts, ground|2 tbsp sweet soy sauce|1 tbsp chili paste|1 tbsp lime juice|2 tbsp water|salt
instructions: Cut each tofu puff in half, creating a pocket.|Stuff with bean sprouts, cucumber and jicama.|Make sauce: mix ground peanuts, sweet soy sauce, chili paste, lime juice and water.|Drizzle peanut sauce generously over the stuffed tofu.|Serve immediately as a snack.

title: Roti Boyan
description: Roti Boyan is a sweet Bruneian flatbread filled with grated coconut and palm sugar, pan-fried until golden — named after the Boyan community.
ingredients: 2 cups all-purpose flour|1/2 cup water|1 tbsp butter|1/4 tsp salt|For filling: 1 cup grated coconut|1/3 cup palm sugar or brown sugar|1/4 tsp cinnamon|butter for frying
instructions: Make dough: flour, water, butter, salt. Knead smooth, rest 20 minutes.|Mix coconut, palm sugar and cinnamon for filling.|Divide dough into 8 balls, roll each thin.|Place filling on one half, fold over and seal edges.|Fry in butter on a griddle 2-3 minutes per side until golden.|Serve warm as a snack or breakfast.

title: Tô Burkina
summary: Burkina Faso stiff millet porridge served with sauce
description: Tô is the everyday staple of Burkina Faso — thick millet or sorghum flour porridge cooked until very stiff, served with a variety of sauces.
ingredients: 2 cups millet flour|4 cups water|1/2 tsp salt
instructions: Bring 3 cups water to a rolling boil.|Mix flour with remaining cold water into a paste.|Pour paste into boiling water, stir vigorously with a wooden spoon.|Cook on medium heat 15-20 minutes, stirring constantly, until very thick and stiff.|Wet a bowl, scoop tô in and shape.|Invert onto a plate, serve with any Burkinabè sauce.

title: Riz Gras Burkina
summary: Burkinabè one-pot tomato rice with meat and vegetables
description: Riz Gras is Burkina Faso's popular rice dish — rice cooked in a rich tomato and meat broth with vegetables, similar to jollof rice.
ingredients: 2 cups rice|400g beef or chicken, cubed|3 tomatoes, blended|1 onion, diced|2 carrots, diced|1/4 cabbage, shredded|3 tbsp vegetable oil|2 tbsp tomato paste|3 cups broth or water|1 tsp salt|1 bouillon cube
instructions: Brown meat in oil, remove.|Fry onion, add blended tomatoes and tomato paste, cook 15 minutes.|Return meat, add broth and bouillon cube.|Add carrots and cabbage, bring to boil.|Add rice, stir once, cover and cook on low 18-20 minutes.|Fluff with fork, season with salt.|Serve hot.

title: Ragout d'Igname
summary: Burkinabè yam stew with meat and tomatoes
description: Ragout d'Igname is a hearty stew of yam cubes simmered with meat, tomatoes and onions — a comforting everyday dish in Burkina Faso.
ingredients: 1 kg yam, peeled and cubed|300g beef, cubed|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp tomato paste|2 tbsp oil|4 cups water|1 bouillon cube|salt and pepper
instructions: Brown beef in oil, add onion, cook 3 minutes.|Add tomatoes and tomato paste, cook 5 minutes.|Add water and bouillon cube, simmer 30 minutes until beef is tender.|Add yam cubes, cook 20 more minutes until yam is soft.|Season with salt and pepper, serve hot.

title: Poulet Bicyclette
description: Poulet Bicyclette (bicycle chicken) is Burkina Faso's iconic free-range chicken, grilled over charcoal and served with tô, attieke or fries — named because the chickens roam free.
ingredients: 1 whole chicken, spatchcocked|3 tbsp vegetable oil|juice of 2 limes|4 cloves garlic, minced|1 onion, grated|1 tsp salt|1 tsp black pepper|1 tsp chili powder|1 bouillon cube, crumbled
instructions: Mix oil, lime juice, garlic, grated onion, salt, pepper, chili and bouillon.|Rub all over the chicken, marinate 2-4 hours.|Grill over medium charcoal, turning frequently, for 45-55 minutes until charred and cooked through.|Chop into pieces.|Serve with attieke, fried plantains or tô with pepper sauce.

title: Haricots en Sauce
description: Haricots en Sauce is a protein-rich Burkinabè dish of red or white beans simmered in a tomato and onion sauce — cheap, filling and delicious.
ingredients: 2 cups dried beans, soaked overnight|2 tomatoes, blended|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp tomato paste|2 tbsp palm oil or vegetable oil|1 bouillon cube|salt|4 cups water
instructions: Boil soaked beans until tender, about 45 minutes.|In a separate pan, heat oil, fry onion until soft.|Add blended tomatoes and tomato paste, cook 10 minutes.|Add cooked beans with some of their water, bouillon cube.|Simmer 15 minutes until sauce is thick.|Season with salt, serve with rice or tô.

title: Mafé Burkina
summary: Burkinabè peanut butter stew with meat
description: Mafé is a West African peanut stew popular in Burkina Faso — meat simmered in a thick, creamy peanut butter and tomato sauce.
ingredients: 500g beef or chicken, cubed|1/2 cup natural peanut butter|3 tomatoes, blended|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp tomato paste|1 carrot, sliced|1 sweet potato, cubed|2 tbsp oil|3 cups water|salt|1 bouillon cube
instructions: Brown meat in oil, add onion, cook 3 minutes.|Add blended tomatoes and tomato paste, cook 10 minutes.|Add water and bouillon cube, simmer 30 minutes.|Dissolve peanut butter in 1 cup warm water, add to the pot.|Add carrot and sweet potato, cook 20 more minutes.|Season with salt, serve over rice.

title: Soumbala Sauce
summary: Burkinabè sauce made with fermented locust bean seasoning
description: Soumbala Sauce uses the pungent fermented locust bean condiment as its base — a deeply umami sauce for tô, unique to West African cuisine.
ingredients: 4 tbsp soumbala (fermented locust beans)|3 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp palm oil|1 scotch bonnet pepper|1 cup water|salt|okra or greens (optional)
instructions: Crumble soumbala beans.|Heat palm oil, fry onion until soft.|Add tomatoes and pepper, cook 10 minutes.|Add soumbala and water, simmer 15 minutes.|Add okra or greens if using, cook 5 more minutes.|Season with salt, serve with tô.

title: Brochettes Burkina
summary: Burkinabè grilled meat skewers served as street food
description: Brochettes are Burkina Faso's ubiquitous street food — cubes of beef, goat or chicken grilled on skewers over charcoal, served with mustard and onions.
ingredients: 500g beef or goat, cubed|1 tbsp oil|1 tsp salt|1 tsp pepper|1 tsp chili powder|1 onion, quartered|skewers|mustard and raw onion for serving
instructions: Season meat with oil, salt, pepper and chili powder.|Thread onto skewers alternating with onion pieces.|Grill over hot charcoal, turning, for 8-10 minutes until charred and cooked.|Serve hot with mustard and sliced raw onion.

title: Dolo Beer
description: Dolo is Burkina Faso's traditional beer brewed from fermented red sorghum — a slightly sour, mildly alcoholic social drink central to community life.
ingredients: 2 kg red sorghum grain|10 liters water|traditional fermentation vessel
instructions: Soak sorghum grains in water for 24 hours, drain.|Spread on mats, keep moist and warm for 3-4 days until they sprout (malting).|Dry the sprouted grain in the sun, then grind.|Mix ground malt with water, boil for several hours in a large pot.|Strain the liquid, cool to warm.|Let ferment naturally for 12-24 hours.|The resulting dolo is cloudy, slightly sour and about 3-4% alcohol.|Serve fresh from a calabash — it doesn't keep long.

title: Benachin Burkina
summary: Burkinabè one-pot rice with fish and vegetables
description: Benachin-style one-pot rice in Burkina Faso features fish or meat cooked with rice, tomatoes and vegetables in a single pot — a festive communal dish.
ingredients: 2 cups rice|4 pieces fish or 400g chicken|3 tomatoes, blended|1 onion, diced|1 eggplant, cubed|1 carrot, sliced|1/4 cabbage, chopped|2 tbsp tomato paste|3 tbsp oil|3 cups water|1 bouillon cube|salt
instructions: Season fish with salt, fry briefly, set aside.|Fry onion, add blended tomatoes and tomato paste, cook 15 minutes.|Add water, bouillon cube and vegetables, bring to boil.|Add rice, stir once, place fish on top.|Cover tightly, cook on low 20 minutes until rice is done.|Serve on a communal platter.

title: Fonio Porridge
summary: Burkinabè ancient grain porridge from fonio millet
description: Fonio is an ancient grain sacred to West Africa — in Burkina Faso it's cooked into a fine, fluffy porridge or couscous served with sauces.
ingredients: 1 cup fonio grain|2 cups water|1/4 tsp salt|1 tbsp butter
instructions: Rinse fonio several times until water runs clear.|Bring water and salt to a boil.|Add fonio, stir, reduce heat to lowest.|Cover and cook 5 minutes.|Remove from heat, fluff with a fork, stir in butter.|Let steam covered 5 more minutes.|Serve as a base for any sauce or stew.

title: Groundnut Soup Burkina
summary: Burkinabè peanut soup with chicken and vegetables
description: Groundnut Soup is a rich Burkinabè soup of pounded peanuts simmered with chicken, tomatoes and okra — thick, nutty and satisfying.
ingredients: 500g chicken pieces|1 cup raw peanuts, roasted and ground|3 tomatoes, blended|1 onion, diced|6 okra pods, sliced|2 tbsp oil|4 cups water|1 bouillon cube|salt|chili pepper
instructions: Brown chicken in oil, add onion, cook 3 minutes.|Add blended tomatoes, cook 10 minutes.|Add water and bouillon cube, simmer 20 minutes.|Stir in ground peanuts until dissolved.|Add okra, simmer 10 more minutes until thick.|Season with salt and chili, serve with rice or tô.

title: Koko Millet Porridge
description: Koko is a smooth, thin millet porridge served for breakfast in Burkina Faso — often flavored with sugar, lemon or ginger.
ingredients: 1 cup millet flour|4 cups water|sugar to taste|1 tsp fresh ginger, grated|juice of 1 lemon (optional)
instructions: Mix millet flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth.|Boil remaining water.|Stir the paste into boiling water, stirring constantly.|Cook 10 minutes on low heat until smooth and thickened.|Add ginger and sugar to taste.|Serve warm with lemon juice if desired.

title: Waafu Rice
summary: Burkinabè fried rice with vegetables and egg
description: Waafu Rice is Burkina Faso's street-style fried rice — day-old rice stir-fried with vegetables, egg and spices, served with grilled meat.
ingredients: 3 cups cooked rice (cooled)|2 eggs|1 onion, diced|1 carrot, diced|1/4 cabbage, shredded|2 tbsp soy sauce|2 tbsp oil|salt and pepper|1 bouillon cube, crumbled
instructions: Heat oil in a large skillet or wok.|Scramble eggs, break into small pieces, set aside.|Fry onion, carrot and cabbage until softened.|Add cold rice, toss on high heat 3-4 minutes.|Add soy sauce and bouillon, toss well.|Return scrambled egg, season with salt and pepper.|Serve hot.

title: Fonio Salad
summary: Burkinabè cold fonio grain salad with vegetables
description: Fonio Salad is a refreshing Burkinabè dish of cooked fonio tossed with fresh vegetables, lemon juice and herbs — light and nutritious.
ingredients: 1 cup fonio, cooked and cooled|2 tomatoes, diced|1 cucumber, diced|1 bell pepper, diced|1/4 cup fresh parsley, chopped|3 tbsp olive oil|juice of 1 lemon|salt and pepper
instructions: Fluff cooled cooked fonio with a fork.|Toss with all diced vegetables and parsley.|Dress with olive oil, lemon juice, salt and pepper.|Serve at room temperature as a salad or light meal.

title: Arachide Grillée
summary: Burkinabè roasted peanuts with salt and chili
description: Arachide Grillée are dry-roasted peanuts sold as street snacks throughout Burkina Faso — simply seasoned with salt and sometimes chili powder.
ingredients: 2 cups raw peanuts in shell or shelled|1 tsp salt|1/2 tsp chili powder (optional)
instructions: Spread peanuts on a baking sheet.|Roast at 160°C (325°F) for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden and fragrant.|Toss with salt and chili powder while hot.|Cool and serve as a snack.

title: Koliko Burkina
description: Koliko in Burkina Faso are fried ripe plantain slices — sweet, caramelized and golden, eaten as a snack or side dish.
ingredients: 4 ripe plantains|oil for frying|salt
instructions: Peel plantains, slice diagonally 1/2 inch thick.|Heat oil to 175°C (350°F).|Fry slices until golden and caramelized, 3-4 minutes.|Drain on paper towels, season lightly with salt.|Serve warm.

title: Gâteau de Sorgho
description: Gâteau de Sorgho is a simple Burkinabè cake made with sorghum flour, sugar and eggs — dense and slightly nutty in flavor.
ingredients: 2 cups sorghum flour|1 cup sugar|4 eggs|1/2 cup vegetable oil|1/2 cup milk|1 tsp baking powder|1/2 tsp vanilla|pinch of salt
instructions: Beat eggs and sugar until fluffy.|Add oil, milk and vanilla, mix.|Fold in sorghum flour, baking powder and salt.|Pour into a greased baking pan.|Bake at 180°C (350°F) for 30-35 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.|Cool, slice and serve.

title: Moringa Soup
description: Moringa Soup uses the leaves of the moringa tree — one of the most nutritious plants in the world — cooked into a simple, healthy Burkinabè soup.
ingredients: 3 cups fresh moringa leaves (or 3 tbsp moringa powder)|1 onion, diced|2 tomatoes, diced|1 tbsp palm oil|1 tsp ground crayfish or bouillon|4 cups water|salt
instructions: Heat palm oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add water and bring to a boil.|Add moringa leaves (or powder), stir.|Add ground crayfish, simmer 10 minutes.|Season with salt, serve with tô or rice.

title: Bissap Drink
description: Bissap is a vibrant red drink made from dried hibiscus flowers — sweet, tart and refreshing, served cold throughout Burkina Faso.
ingredients: 1 cup dried hibiscus flowers (bissap)|6 cups water|1/2 cup sugar|1 tsp vanilla extract|juice of 1 lemon|fresh mint leaves|ice
instructions: Bring water to a boil, add hibiscus flowers.|Simmer 10 minutes, then remove from heat.|Let steep 30 minutes.|Strain, pressing flowers to extract all color and flavor.|Add sugar, lemon juice and vanilla, stir until dissolved.|Chill thoroughly.|Serve over ice with fresh mint.

title: Isombe Burundi
summary: Burundian cassava leaf stew with palm oil and peanuts
description: Isombe is Burundi's beloved dish of pounded cassava leaves cooked with palm oil, ground peanuts and sometimes eggplant — earthy, rich and deeply satisfying.
ingredients: 500g cassava leaves, pounded (or frozen)|1/4 cup palm oil|1/4 cup peanut butter or ground peanuts|1 eggplant, cubed|1 onion, diced|2 tomatoes, diced|1 clove garlic|salt|4 cups water
instructions: Boil pounded cassava leaves in water for 20 minutes, drain.|Heat palm oil, sauté onion and garlic.|Add tomatoes and eggplant, cook 10 minutes.|Add cassava leaves and peanut butter, stir well.|Simmer 20-30 minutes until thick and flavors meld.|Season with salt, serve with rice or ubugali.

title: Umutsima
summary: Burundian cassava and corn flour porridge
description: Umutsima is Burundi's everyday starchy staple — a thick, smooth porridge made by cooking cassava flour and corn flour together until stiff.
ingredients: 1 cup cassava flour|1 cup corn flour|4 cups water|1/2 tsp salt
instructions: Bring water to a boil with salt.|Mix both flours with a little cold water to make a paste.|Pour paste into boiling water, stirring vigorously.|Cook on medium heat 15-20 minutes, stirring constantly, until very thick.|Shape into a mound, serve with stew or beans.

title: Ibiharage
summary: Burundian red kidney beans in tomato sauce
description: Ibiharage (beans) are the cornerstone of Burundian diet — red kidney beans slowly simmered with tomatoes, onion and palm oil until thick and creamy.
ingredients: 2 cups red kidney beans, soaked overnight|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp palm oil|salt|4 cups water
instructions: Boil soaked beans in fresh water until tender, about 1 hour.|Heat palm oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add cooked beans with some of their liquid.|Simmer 15-20 minutes until sauce is thick.|Season with salt, serve with ubugali or rice.

title: Urwarwa Banana Beer
description: Urwarwa is Burundi's traditional banana beer — ripe bananas are fermented with sorghum flour into a mildly alcoholic, slightly sour beverage shared from a communal pot.
ingredients: 10 kg ripe bananas|1 cup sorghum flour|water|grass for filtering
instructions: Ripen bananas until skins are very dark.|Peel and mash thoroughly (traditionally by foot in a hollowed log).|Mix in sorghum flour and a little water.|Let ferment for 24-48 hours, stirring occasionally.|Filter through clean grass to separate liquid from pulp.|The resulting beer is cloudy, sweet-sour, about 5% alcohol.|Serve fresh from a shared container using reed straws.

title: Mandazi Burundi
description: Mandazi in Burundi are slightly sweet fried dough triangles flavored with coconut milk and cardamom — eaten for breakfast or as a snack.
ingredients: 2 cups flour|1/3 cup sugar|1/2 cup coconut milk|1 egg|1 tsp baking powder|1/4 tsp cardamom|pinch of salt|oil for frying
instructions: Mix flour, sugar, baking powder, cardamom and salt.|Add egg and coconut milk, mix into a soft dough.|Knead briefly, rest 15 minutes.|Roll out 1/2 inch thick, cut into triangles.|Fry in 170°C (340°F) oil until golden on both sides, about 3 minutes.|Drain on paper towels, serve warm.

title: Matoke Burundi
description: Matoke is a Burundian dish of green cooking bananas peeled, wrapped in banana leaves and steamed or stewed with tomatoes and onions until soft and savory.
ingredients: 8 green bananas (matoke/plantains), peeled|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp palm oil|1 cup water|salt|banana leaves for wrapping (optional)
instructions: Peel green bananas, place in a pot.|Add onion, tomatoes, palm oil and water.|Cover with banana leaves or a tight lid.|Cook on low heat for 30-40 minutes until bananas are very soft.|Mash lightly with a fork.|Season with salt, serve as a main starch.

title: Ubugali
description: Ubugali is the Burundian version of ugali — thick, smooth cassava flour porridge, the standard accompaniment to every Burundian meal.
ingredients: 2 cups cassava flour|4 cups water|1/2 tsp salt
instructions: Bring water with salt to a boil.|Gradually add cassava flour while stirring vigorously.|Cook on medium heat, stirring constantly, for 10-15 minutes until very thick and smooth.|Shape into a mound on a plate.|Serve with beans, isombe or any stew.

title: Brochettes de Boeuf Burundi
summary: Burundian grilled beef skewers with banana and vegetables
description: Brochettes de Boeuf are Burundi's favorite street food — cubes of beef grilled on skewers over charcoal, often alternating with banana and pepper pieces.
ingredients: 500g beef, cubed|2 green bananas, cut into chunks|1 bell pepper, cubed|1 onion, quartered|2 tbsp oil|salt and pepper|chili powder|skewers
instructions: Season beef with oil, salt, pepper and chili.|Thread beef alternating with banana, pepper and onion onto skewers.|Grill over hot charcoal 8-10 minutes, turning frequently.|Serve with rice and chili sauce.

title: Agatogo
summary: Burundian green banana and vegetable stew
description: Agatogo is a Burundian stew of green bananas cooked with a mix of vegetables, sometimes with meat, in a lightly seasoned broth.
ingredients: 6 green bananas, peeled and halved|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|1 carrot, sliced|1/4 cabbage, chopped|2 tbsp oil|2 cups water|salt|1 bouillon cube
instructions: Heat oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add bananas, carrot, cabbage, water and bouillon.|Simmer 25-30 minutes until bananas are tender.|Season with salt, serve hot.

title: Amaranth Stew
summary: Burundian amaranth leaf stew with tomatoes
description: Amaranth leaves are widely eaten in Burundi as a nutritious green — sautéed with tomatoes, onions and palm oil into a simple vitamin-rich stew.
ingredients: 500g amaranth leaves, chopped|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp palm oil|1 clove garlic, minced|salt
instructions: Heat palm oil, sauté onion and garlic until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add amaranth leaves, stir.|Cover and cook 10-15 minutes until leaves are tender.|Season with salt, serve with ubugali or rice.

title: Cornmeal Ugali Burundi
description: Cornmeal ugali is made when cassava is not available — fine cornmeal cooked into a firm, smooth porridge for scooping up stews and sauces.
ingredients: 2 cups fine cornmeal|4 cups water|salt
instructions: Bring water and salt to a boil.|Gradually pour in cornmeal, stirring constantly.|Cook on low heat 15 minutes, stirring, until very thick.|Shape into a mound, serve with bean stew or greens.

title: Red Kidney Bean Soup Burundi
description: A thick, soupy version of ibiharage — red kidney beans cooked until they start to break down into a thick, nourishing soup, a Burundian comfort food.
ingredients: 2 cups red kidney beans, soaked overnight|1 onion, diced|2 tomatoes, diced|2 tbsp oil|6 cups water|salt
instructions: Boil beans until very soft, about 1.5 hours, until some start to break down.|Sauté onion in oil, add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add to the beans, simmer 15 minutes.|Season with salt. The soup should be thick with broken-down beans.|Serve in bowls with bread or ubugali.

title: Fried Tilapia Burundi
summary: Burundian whole fried Lake Tanganyika tilapia
description: Freshwater tilapia from Lake Tanganyika is simply seasoned with salt and lemon, fried whole until crispy — a Burundian lakeside specialty.
ingredients: 4 whole tilapia, cleaned and scored|juice of 2 lemons|1 tsp salt|1/2 tsp black pepper|oil for frying|lemon wedges and chili sauce for serving
instructions: Score fish on both sides, rub with lemon juice, salt and pepper.|Let marinate 15 minutes.|Heat oil in a deep pan.|Fry fish 5-6 minutes per side until golden and crispy.|Drain on paper towels.|Serve with lemon wedges, chili sauce and rice.

title: Sweet Potato Stew Burundi
summary: Burundian sweet potatoes simmered with onion and tomato
description: Sweet potatoes are a staple in Burundi — here they're cubed and simmered with tomatoes and onion into a simple, comforting stew.
ingredients: 4 large sweet potatoes, peeled and cubed|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp oil|2 cups water|salt
instructions: Heat oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add sweet potatoes and water.|Simmer 20-25 minutes until sweet potatoes are tender.|Season with salt, serve as a side or main.

title: Cassava Leaves Burundi
description: Cassava leaves are a major green vegetable in Burundi — pounded and slow-cooked until tender, simply seasoned with palm oil and salt.
ingredients: 500g cassava leaves, pounded or frozen|2 tbsp palm oil|1 onion, diced|salt|3 cups water
instructions: Boil pounded cassava leaves in water for 30 minutes to remove bitterness.|Drain and squeeze out excess water.|Heat palm oil, sauté onion.|Add cassava leaves, stir.|Cook 15 minutes on low heat until tender and well seasoned.|Season with salt, serve as a vegetable side.

title: Plantain Soup Burundi
description: A simple Burundian soup of green plantains simmered until soft in a lightly seasoned broth — filling and starchy.
ingredients: 4 green plantains, peeled and sliced|1 onion, diced|2 tomatoes, diced|4 cups water|1 tbsp oil|salt
instructions: Heat oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 3 minutes.|Add plantain slices and water.|Simmer 25 minutes until plantains are very soft.|Mash some plantains with a spoon to thicken the soup.|Season with salt, serve warm.

title: Baked Sweet Potato Burundi
description: Roasted sweet potatoes are one of the simplest and most common foods in Burundi — whole sweet potatoes buried in hot coals until tender and caramelized.
ingredients: 4 large sweet potatoes|salt (optional)
instructions: Build a charcoal or wood fire, let it burn down to hot coals.|Bury whole sweet potatoes in the hot ashes.|Roast for 30-45 minutes, turning once.|They are done when soft when squeezed.|Brush off ash, split open, eat plain or with a pinch of salt.

title: Mango Salad Burundi
summary: Burundian fresh mango salad with lime and chili
description: A simple Burundian salad of ripe mango slices tossed with lime juice, chili and a pinch of salt — refreshing and commonly sold as a street snack.
ingredients: 3 ripe mangoes, peeled and sliced|juice of 2 limes|1/4 tsp chili powder|pinch of salt
instructions: Arrange mango slices on a plate.|Squeeze lime juice over the mangoes.|Sprinkle with chili powder and salt.|Toss gently, serve immediately.

title: Pea Stew Burundi
summary: Burundian green pea stew with onion and tomato
description: Green peas stewed with tomatoes and onion — a simple, nutritious Burundian side dish that complements any meal.
ingredients: 2 cups green peas (fresh or frozen)|2 tomatoes, diced|1 onion, diced|1 tbsp oil|1 cup water|salt
instructions: Heat oil, sauté onion until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.|Add peas and water.|Simmer 15-20 minutes until peas are tender.|Season with salt, serve as a side.

title: Inyama ya Ingwe
description: Inyama ya Ingwe is a festive Burundian goat stew — chunks of goat meat slowly braised with onions, tomatoes and simple seasonings until meltingly tender.
ingredients: 1 kg goat meat, cubed|2 onions, sliced|3 tomatoes, diced|2 tbsp oil|1 tsp salt|1/2 tsp pepper|4 cups water
instructions: Brown goat meat in oil on all sides.|Add onions, cook until softened.|Add tomatoes, salt and pepper, stir.|Add water, bring to boil, then reduce to a simmer.|Cook covered for 2-3 hours until meat is very tender.|The sauce should reduce to a thick gravy.|Serve with rice or ubugali.

title: Ndolé
summary: Cameroon's national dish of bitter leaves with peanuts and meat
description: Ndolé is Cameroon's most famous dish — bitter leaves cooked with ground peanuts, shrimp and meat into a thick, complex stew served with plantains.
ingredients: 500g bitter leaves (or kale/spinach substitute)|1/2 cup ground peanuts|300g beef, cubed|200g smoked shrimp or crayfish|2 onions, diced|3 cloves garlic, minced|2 tomatoes, diced|3 tbsp palm oil|salt|cooked plantains for serving
instructions: Boil bitter leaves in several changes of water to remove bitterness, squeeze dry.|Brown beef in palm oil, add onion and garlic, cook until soft.|Add tomatoes, cook 10 minutes.|Add enough water to cover meat, simmer 30 minutes until tender.|Add ground peanuts, stir until dissolved.|Add the prepared bitter leaves and smoked shrimp.|Simmer 20 minutes until thick and flavors meld.|Season with salt, serve with fried or boiled plantains.

title: Eru Cameroon
summary: Cameroonian wild greens stewed with waterleaf and palm oil
description: Eru is a pungent Cameroonian stew of shredded eru leaves and waterleaf cooked with palm oil, stockfish, crayfish and cow skin — an Anglophone Cameroon favorite.
ingredients: 300g eru leaves, finely shredded|200g waterleaf or spinach, chopped|200g dried stockfish, soaked|200g cow skin (kanda), boiled|1/4 cup ground crayfish|1/2 cup palm oil|salt
instructions: Soak stockfish until soft, break into pieces. Boil cow skin until tender, cut into pieces.|Wash eru leaves but do not cook them separately.|Heat palm oil in a pot (do not bleach it).|Add stockfish, cow skin and ground crayfish, cook 10 minutes.|Add waterleaf, cook until wilted, about 5 minutes.|Add shredded eru leaves, stir well.|Cook on low heat 15-20 minutes — do NOT add water, the waterleaf provides moisture.|Season with salt, serve with garri (or fufu).

title: Kondré
summary: Cameroonian unripe plantain and goat meat stew
description: Kondré is a Cameroonian specialty of green plantains cooked with goat meat in a peppery sauce — a hearty, comforting one-pot dish.
ingredients: 6 green plantains, peeled and halved|500g goat meat, cubed|2 onions, diced|3 tomatoes, diced|2 tbsp tomato paste|3 tbsp palm oil|1 scotch bonnet pepper|4 cups water|salt|1 bouillon cube
instructions: Brown goat meat in palm oil, add onions, cook 5 minutes.|Add tomatoes, tomato paste and scotch bonnet, cook 10 minutes.|Add water and bouillon, simmer 40 minutes until goat is tender.|Add green plantains, cook 25 more minutes until plantains are soft.|Season with salt, serve hot.

title: Bobolo
summary: Cameroonian fermented cassava sticks wrapped in leaves
description: Bobolo are Cameroonian fermented cassava rolls — cassava dough is fermented, shaped into sticks, wrapped in banana leaves and steamed, eaten with any stew.
ingredients: 2 kg cassava, peeled|water for soaking|banana leaves for wrapping
instructions: Soak cassava in water for 3-4 days until soft and fermented.|Remove fibers, pound or blend into a smooth paste.|Shape the paste into thick sticks about 8 inches long.|Wrap each stick tightly in banana leaves, tie with string.|Steam for 1 hour until firm.|Serve alongside ndolé, eru or any sauce — peel off the leaf and eat.

title: Ekwang
summary: Cameroonian grated cocoyam and spinach rolls in palm oil
description: Ekwang is a Cameroonian delicacy from the Southwest — grated cocoyam wrapped in cocoyam leaves, cooked in palm oil with crayfish and smoked fish.
ingredients: 1 kg cocoyam (taro), peeled and grated|cocoyam or spinach leaves|200g smoked fish|1/4 cup ground crayfish|1/2 cup palm oil|1 scotch bonnet pepper|salt|1 cup water
instructions: Grate cocoyam into a fine paste.|Spread a tablespoon of paste onto each cocoyam or spinach leaf, roll up tightly.|Pack the rolls snugly in a pot.|Add palm oil, ground crayfish, smoked fish, scotch bonnet and water.|Cover tightly, cook on low heat for 1-1.5 hours until cocoyam is cooked through.|Season with salt, serve hot.

title: Koki Beans
summary: Cameroonian steamed bean pudding wrapped in banana leaves
description: Koki is a Cameroonian dish of blended black-eyed peas mixed with palm oil and spices, wrapped in banana leaves and steamed into a savory pudding.
ingredients: 2 cups black-eyed peas, soaked and skinned|1/2 cup palm oil|1 scotch bonnet pepper|1 onion, diced|salt|banana leaves for wrapping|string for tying
instructions: Blend skinned black-eyed peas into a smooth paste.|Mix in palm oil, minced scotch bonnet, diced onion and salt.|Beat the mixture vigorously until light and fluffy.|Spoon generous amounts onto banana leaf squares, fold into parcels and tie.|Steam for 1.5-2 hours until firm.|Unwrap and serve warm — the texture should be firm like a savory pudding.

title: Mbanga Soup
summary: Cameroonian palm nut soup with meat and fish
description: Mbanga Soup (Banga Soup) is made from pressed palm fruit extract, cooked with meat, stockfish and spices into a rich, oily, flavorful Cameroonian soup.
ingredients: 2 cups palm fruit extract or 1 can palm cream|500g goat or beef, cubed|200g stockfish, soaked|1 onion, diced|2 scotch bonnet peppers|1 tbsp ground crayfish|okok leaves or spinach (optional)|4 cups water|salt
instructions: Boil meat in water until tender, about 40 minutes.|Add palm cream, scotch bonnet, onion and crayfish.|Simmer 30 minutes until oil rises and soup thickens.|Add soaked stockfish and greens if using.|Cook 10 more minutes, season with salt.|Serve with bobolo, miondo or fufu.

title: Sanga Cameroon
description: Sanga is a Cameroonian dish of corn cooked with beans and vegetables into a thick, hearty porridge — a filling one-pot meal from the western highlands.
ingredients: 2 cups dried corn, soaked overnight|1 cup red beans, soaked|1/2 cup palm oil|2 cups pumpkin or squash, cubed|1 cup greens (huckleberry or spinach)|salt|6 cups water
instructions: Boil soaked corn and beans together for 1.5-2 hours until tender.|Add pumpkin cubes, cook 15 minutes.|Add palm oil and greens, stir.|Cook 10 more minutes until thick and well combined.|Season with salt, serve hot in bowls.

title: Jollof Rice Cameroon
summary: Cameroonian one-pot tomato rice with chicken
description: Cameroonian Jollof Rice is a beloved party dish — rice cooked in a rich tomato and chili sauce with chicken, vegetables and aromatic spices.
ingredients: 3 cups rice|500g chicken pieces|4 tomatoes, blended|1 onion, diced|2 tbsp tomato paste|1 scotch bonnet pepper|1 tsp thyme|2 bay leaves|1/4 cup vegetable oil|3 cups chicken broth|salt
